title: SQL Advanced Optimization & Execution Plans
description: Execution plan analysis, advanced optimization techniques, statistics, and query hints.

questions.text: What is a query execution plan?

options.text: A written document describing database design
The sequence of physical operations the query optimizer chooses to execute a SQL query
A list of indexes defined on a table
The stored procedure cache

questions.text: In a graphical execution plan in SQL Server Management Studio, in which direction do you read operators?

options.text: Left to right
Top to bottom
Right to left
Bottom to top

questions.text: What does a thick arrow between two operators in an execution plan indicate?

options.text: A fast join operation
A large number of rows flowing between those operators
A high CPU cost operation
A network transfer between servers

questions.text: What is the difference between an Estimated and an Actual execution plan?

options.text: They are always identical
An estimated plan is generated without running the query; an actual plan is generated after execution and includes runtime statistics like actual row counts
An actual plan is less accurate than an estimated plan
Estimated plans include index usage; actual plans do not

questions.text: What does it mean when an execution plan shows a large difference between 'Estimated Rows' and 'Actual Rows'?

options.text: The query is running correctly
Statistics are likely out of date, causing the optimizer to make poor plan choices
The index is corrupted
The query is returning too many rows

questions.text: What is a Table Scan vs. an Index Seek in execution plans?

options.text: Table Scan navigates the B-tree directly; Index Seek reads all pages
Table Scan reads every row in the table (no usable index); Index Seek navigates the B-tree to find specific rows efficiently
They are equivalent operations
Index Seek is only used for full-text search

questions.text: What is a Key Lookup (Clustered) in an execution plan, and when is it a performance concern?

options.text: It is always efficient and not a concern
After a non-clustered index seek, the engine fetches additional columns from the clustered index; it becomes costly when applied to many rows
It is a join between two clustered tables
It looks up the primary key value in a foreign key table

questions.text: What is parameter sniffing and when does it cause problems?

options.text: SQL Server detects missing parameters automatically and causes compilation errors
SQL Server uses the parameter values from the first execution to compile and cache a plan; this is problematic if later executions use very different parameter values with different optimal plans
A security feature that logs parameter values
Automatic parameter detection that always improves performance

questions.text: Which query hint forces a new execution plan to be compiled for each execution, solving parameter sniffing issues?

options.text: OPTION (OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN)
OPTION (RECOMPILE)
OPTION (MAXDOP 1)
WITH (NOLOCK)

questions.text: What does MAXDOP control in query hints?

options.text: The maximum number of rows returned
The maximum degree of parallelism — the number of CPU cores used for a parallel query
The maximum time a query can run
The maximum number of joins allowed

questions.text: What does OPTION (MAXDOP 1) force?

options.text: The query to use all available CPUs
The query to run single-threaded with no parallelism
The query to use 1 GB of memory
The query to recompile once

questions.text: What are statistics in SQL Server and why are they important?

options.text: System tables that list all indexes
Objects containing histogram data about the distribution of column values, used by the query optimizer to estimate row counts and choose efficient plans
Performance counters for server CPU usage
Query execution history logs

questions.text: What command updates statistics on a table in SQL Server?

options.text: REFRESH STATISTICS TableName
UPDATE STATISTICS TableName
REBUILD STATISTICS TableName
RECALCULATE STATISTICS TableName

questions.text: What is the difference between a temp table (#temp) and a table variable (@tableVar) in terms of statistics?

options.text: Table variables have full statistics; temp tables do not
Temp tables have statistics and can be indexed like regular tables; table variables have no statistics and the optimizer always estimates 1 row
They are identical in terms of statistics
Table variables are always faster than temp tables

questions.text: When would you use a temp table over a table variable?

options.text: For small datasets with a few rows
For larger datasets where statistics and indexes are needed for subsequent queries, improving optimizer accuracy
Table variables are always preferable
When the data should persist after the session ends

questions.text: What is tempdb used for in SQL Server?

options.text: Permanent storage for user databases
A system database used for temporary tables, table variables, sort/hash operations, row versioning, and internal worktables
Storing transaction logs
Backing up user databases

questions.text: What does the NOLOCK table hint do and what risk does it carry?

options.text: Prevents all writes to the table; risk is reduced write performance
Reads data without acquiring shared locks, improving read concurrency but risking dirty reads of uncommitted data
Locks the table exclusively; risk is blocking other reads
Removes all existing locks; risk is data corruption

questions.text: What does the READPAST hint do?

options.text: Reads all locked rows regardless of locks
Skips rows that are currently locked by other transactions instead of waiting
Forces the query to read from a past point in time
Reads rows in reverse order

questions.text: What is the UPDLOCK hint used for?

options.text: Prevents the table from being updated
Acquires an update lock during a read, signaling intent to update and preventing deadlocks in read-then-update patterns
Forces the query to update all rows it reads
Bypasses the update trigger

questions.text: What is Query Store in SQL Server?

options.text: A storage location for creating queries
A feature that captures query texts, plans, and runtime statistics over time, enabling plan forcing and regression analysis
A cache of recently executed queries
A tool for writing stored procedures

questions.text: What does 'forcing a plan' in Query Store allow you to do?

options.text: Force the query to return specific results
Instruct SQL Server to always use a specific previously captured execution plan for a query, bypassing the optimizer
Force all queries to run in parallel
Force index rebuilds before each execution

questions.text: How do you identify blocking queries in SQL Server?

options.text: SELECT * FROM sys.indexes
Query sys.dm_exec_requests and sys.dm_os_waiting_tasks to find sessions with blocking_session_id populated
Use SET STATISTICS IO ON
Query sys.databases

questions.text: Which DMV can be used to identify the top CPU-consuming queries in SQL Server?

options.text: sys.dm_exec_sessions
sys.dm_exec_query_stats ordered by total_worker_time
sys.dm_os_performance_counters
sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats

questions.text: What causes a Hash Match join in an execution plan, and is it always bad?

options.text: Hash match occurs on indexed columns only; it is always efficient
Hash match is used when joining on unindexed or unsorted columns; it is not inherently bad but can be costly for large datasets due to memory spills to tempdb
Hash match always indicates a missing index
Hash match only occurs on text columns

questions.text: What is a Nested Loops join in an execution plan?

options.text: A join algorithm that builds a hash table for one input and probes it with the other
A join algorithm where for each row in the outer input, it seeks matching rows in the inner input — efficient when the outer set is small and the inner has a good index
A join that sorts both inputs and merges them
A join that only works on equal column values

questions.text: What is a Merge Join in an execution plan?

options.text: A join that merges two tables into one permanent table
A join that requires both inputs to be sorted on the join columns; it then merges them linearly — efficient for large sorted data sets
A join that creates a hash table
A join used only for FULL OUTER JOIN

questions.text: What is a memory spill (worktable spill) in an execution plan and why is it harmful?

options.text: When query results are spilled to a log file — harmful because it fills the log
When a sort, hash, or other memory-intensive operation does not fit in granted memory and overflows to tempdb on disk, causing significant I/O overhead
When a query uses too many temp tables
When a query returns too many rows to the client

questions.text: How does OPTION (OPTIMIZE FOR (@param UNKNOWN)) help with parameter sniffing?

options.text: It disables parameter sniffing entirely
It instructs the optimizer to use average statistical values rather than the supplied parameter value when compiling the plan, producing a more general plan
It forces a recompile on every execution
It caches the plan for the unknown parameter type only

questions.text: Which sys view in SQL Server helps identify long-running currently executing queries?

options.text: sys.dm_exec_requests joined with sys.dm_exec_sql_text to get query text and start time
sys.databases
sys.procedures
sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats

questions.text: What is the purpose of the DBCC SHOW_STATISTICS command?

options.text: Shows the database file sizes
Displays the histogram, density vector, and header information for a statistics object on a table, used to diagnose estimation problems
Shows the execution plan of a query
Displays index fragmentation levels
